None of these pages contain the requested article.

articles.title: Theology and Testimony
articles.complete_text:
Guide Lessons for October

LESSON I
Theology and Testimony
(First Week in October)
PROJECTS CONCLUDED

I. The Adjustability of Projects.

Gospel aims are eternal. The joy of man is the aim of his existence. It is a divine purpose, an everlasting objective in the mind of the Eternal. The Book of Mormon declares the existence of man to be joy (II Nephi 2:25) and Jesus set forth the same truth when he said, "I am that ye might have life and have it more abundantly." (John 10:10). The word "joy" is the shortest and most comprehensive symbol of the abundant life. Joy is a kind of happiness of which it may be said, "once happy, always happy." It is enjoyment, the anticipation of which is happiness, the presence of which is happiness and the memory of which is happiness.

All purposes pointing toward; and all projects pushing along lines not parallel with the great gospel aim, lead away from the abundant life; and the farther one goes on one of these tangent lines, the more abject the poverty of life. Projects for lowering standards are always bad.

The projects for reaching the ever-present and truth-anchored aim, the abundant life, have been expanded, modified, and changed to meet conditions. Note the growth and adjustability of the great missionary project—one in purpose but many in its means and methods—once a few men without purse or scrip, now men and women, thousands of them, carrying the message of life, spiritual life, moral life, intellectual life, and physical life, in rich abundance. Once limited to holding conversation by the wayside as did Jesus at the well, (John 6) now speaking to millions over the radio, as did the Father over his radio to the Nephites when he introduced to them the resurrected Redeemer. (III Nephi 11.) Once the missionaries walked the dusty road as did the apostles with Jesus; now we hear of them outspeeding the wind above the clouds to fill a missionary appointment.

Gospel projects are the machinery of salvation and must be adjustable to the people and to their conditions. They are the roadway to reach the top of the mountain of joy and must curve and wind to make a grade suited to the climber's capacity.

The onwardness of a project may include temporary halts as well as the making of detours. The line of march which Jehovah marked out for ancient Israel under the leadership of Moses is vividly illustrative of the necessity of project adjustability. Forty years they were getting from Egypt to Jericho, a distance which a camel can walk in a few days.

II. The Fruits of Projects.

This is not only a great listening age, but an age of looking as well. We still go to the heart-beat for information, but we use the X-rays also. The telephone communication is not enough, we want television. The project of producing Christian character must result in meeting the condition of the hour when the thinking world is more interested in the fruits than in the roots of the tree life, when doing is of more importance than doctrine. And this condition is in perfect keeping with the leadership of him who said, "by their fruits ye shall know them."

When this dispensation sign of salvation was first put up, the world threw rocks at it and smeared it with mud; its showcase was empty. Now at the close of its first century of existence, its windows blaze with diamonds. They are lent to the world, and glisten from high places in government, in education, in the professions and industries; they sparkle on the belt of middle class leadership; so that far and wide when one asks, "What good comes from 'Mormonism'?" the answer is read, "Look and see!"

Judged by the samples sent out, the Christian character making power of this gospel dispensation, is indeed a "marvel and a wonder." These samples are not the product of any one project of the dispensation, but rather the out-put of many projects: home projects, Priesthood projects, Relief Society projects, Sunday School projects, Church School projects, Mutual Improvement projects, Primary projects, and Genealogical projects; each with its specific purposes and all with a common interest and a universal aim; each in competition with itself and in cooperation with all the others.

The high aims, the superior operative characteristics of its organization, and the effectiveness of its projects, are indisputable evidence that Latter-day Saint Church building is character building; it is Christian character building, for it is a kingdom building, that of this world, but not of this world alone.

III. The Home Beautiful Project.

In the production of character, three great forces are constantly operative: example, instruction, and environment, and of the environment the home is fundamental.

In the order of progress, we have the home protective, then the home plentiful, then the home comfortable, then the home beautiful. The scenic beauty of our Zion's center of population has put the homes of the Saints in the show case of the world, as the endless procession of tourists pass along our highways; Zion is known by her buildings and their surroundings. It must not be forgotten that paint spells prosperity, and that well-kept yards smile and tell of culture.

The home beautiful is an essential part of the Zion building. We sing, "Let Zion in her beauty rise," but the home beautifier says, "See Zion in her beauty rise." The home beautiful is called for by the example of the Church in its temple buildings and the temple grounds. Each of the houses of the Lord has its individual architecture and its fitting surrounding, but they are all alike in being beautiful. The influence of example is added to by instruction; and these two, reinforced by project-activity, reach objective with the greatest speed.

Industrial interests have projects for home-building; commercial interests have projects for home conveniences, comforts, and luxuries; and these projects are pushed to the limit of safety.

And some efforts are being made in the line of education in the direction of having beautiful homes in beautiful settings—that our Zion may be a fitting symbol of the bride, not only in its character but in its apparel also. Every L. D. S. home is a part of the attire of the Church.

Questions and Problems

1. Why must aims be fixed?
2. Why must projects be adjustable?
3. Wherein is joy more than happiness?
4. Discuss the demand of the world to see results rather than to listen to gospel doctrine?
5. What are the indisputable proofs that Latter-day Saint Church-building is a form of superior Christian character-building?
6. Speak five minutes on the topic, "It pays to paint."
7. How do Latter-day Saint temples and temple grounds point to the truth that beautification is an essential part of Zion-building?
8. In what respect is every Latter-day Saint home a part of the attire of the Church?
9. Discuss (a) The need of a home beautiful project in the Church; (b) The organization best adapted to put such a project on foot and put it over.
articles.notes: Found complete Lesson I 'Theology and Testimony' from Guide Lessons for October section. The text is clearly readable and appears to be the complete lesson as it transitions to the next lesson at the end.

articles.title: Work and Business
articles.complete_text:
WORK AND BUSINESS

Topic for November: "The Dignity of Labor."

References: Gospel Doctrine, pages 285-6; Doctrine and Covenants 42:42; 56:17; 68:30-32; 75:3; Discourses of Brigham Young, pages 232-3.

Questions for Discussion:

1. What is the attitude of the Church toward work?

2. What is meant by "the dignity of labor?"

3. What are some of the evils of idleness?

4. How may we make our work a blessing instead of a burden?

5. What is the proper attitude toward those who work with their hands?

6. How may we teach our children to love work?

7. What are some of the occupations that are especially suitable for women?

8. How may a woman make her home work more interesting and efficient?

9. What is the Church attitude toward women working outside the home?

10. How may we help to solve the unemployment problem?

Suggestions to Leaders:

This lesson should emphasize the gospel teaching that work is honorable and necessary. All honest labor is dignified. The Lord has commanded that we work. Idleness leads to temptation and sin. We should teach our children to work and to take pride in doing their work well. Women have many opportunities for useful service both in the home and outside. The home is woman's first responsibility, but circumstances may make it necessary or advisable for her to work outside the home. In such cases she should choose work that is suitable and that will not interfere with her home duties more than necessary. We should all do our part to help solve the unemployment problem by being considerate of those who serve us and by creating work when possible.
articles.notes: Found the complete Work and Business guide lesson on page 502. It includes the topic, references, discussion questions, and suggestions to leaders.
